docs.title: Relationship to firescript
docs.text:
fcl is designed as a constrained subset of firescript concepts:

  • You can define structured data using explicit types.
  • You can define class-based typed structures and typed variables in fcl files.
  • fcl data is accessible from firescript.
  • The language remains intentionally focused on configuration and predictable static structure.

This keeps fcl familiar to firescript users while preserving a clear config-oriented scope.

docs.title: Usage Modes
docs.text:
fcl is planned to support two major modes:

  • Compile-time mode: fcl files are imported and consumed during compilation.
  • Runtime mode (future): fcl files are read through an fcl interpreter.

For runtime usage, schema is defined as a class in the firescript program. Runtime-loaded fcl data is validated against that class so structure and types remain explicit. Runtime file loading is expected to go through standard library file I/O APIs (for example @firescript/std.fs) instead of direct syscall use in application code.

docs.title: Data Model and Expression Layer
docs.text: fcl follows a typed object graph model with a small expression layer. Files are primarily declarative, while still allowing constrained expressions inside values.

docs.title: Example fcl Files
docs.text: The examples below are illustrative and intended to show the design direction.

docs.title: Basic static configuration
docs.text:
class AppConfig {
    string appName = "firescript-app"
    int32 port = 8080
    bool enableTelemetry = false
    [string] allowedHosts = ["localhost", "127.0.0.1"]
}

docs.title: Using deterministic expression helpers
docs.text:
class BuildConfig {
    string mode = (optEnv("BUILD_MODE") ?? "debug")
    string outputDir = pathJoin("build", mode)
    int32 workerCount = parseInt((optEnv("WORKERS") ?? "4"))
}

docs.title: Conditional expression in a value
docs.text:
class RuntimeConfig {
    string environment = (optEnv("ENV") ?? "dev")
    string logLevel = if (environment == "prod") { "warn" } else { "debug" } // Note: expression form of if/else is not currently implemented and may be different syntax when added.
}

docs.title: Class-based runtime loading (firescript)
docs.text:
When loading fcl at runtime, define the expected structure as a class in firescript:

class RuntimeConfig {
    string environment;
    string logLevel;
    int32 port;
}

RuntimeConfig cfg = load_config<RuntimeConfig>("config.fcl");

docs.title: Allowed
docs.text:
• Literals ("text", numeric literals, true, false, null)
  • Object/class initializers for config structures
  • Arrays and maps
  • if/else expressions (expression form, not statement form)
  • Null coalescing (??)
  • Optional ternary expression (?:) if adopted
  • A small set of built-in pure functions (for example env(), optEnv(), pathJoin(), parseInt())
  • Imports of types only, or config-class-only imports

docs.title: Not Allowed
docs.text:
• Defining functions
  • Calling arbitrary methods
  • Loops (for, while)
  • Mutation or reassignment (config values are effectively const)
  • General module-system behavior or arbitrary code execution

docs.title: Logic and Execution Roadmap
docs.text:
fcl is declarative first, but limited logic is planned over time:

  • Basic logic support is planned for future versions.
  • Runtime interpretation is planned after compile-time workflows.
  • Longer term, this roadmap may enable JIT-style execution paths for fcl.

These capabilities are intended to evolve without changing fcl's core identity as a typed configuration format for firescript.

docs.title: Name and Extension
docs.text:
The official name is firescript config language.

  • The short form is fcl.
  • The file extension is .fcl.
  • fcl remains focused on being a firescript-native alternative to YAML/TOML for configuration.

docs.title: kiln Manifest and Configuration Format
docs.text:
Status: [PLANNED]. The kiln manifest format is FCL (firescript config language), which is itself in development. The exact manifest schema below is illustrative design direction and will be finalized alongside kiln in the 0.10.0 release. (An earlier design used TOML; that has been superseded by FCL.)

The firescript package manager, kiln, uses FCL for its configuration. The primary file is kiln.fcl, which must be located at the root of a kiln-managed project alongside the source code.

docs.title: Package Manifest (kiln.fcl)
docs.text:
The kiln.fcl file defines your project's metadata, dependencies, and build profiles. It acts as the source of truth for the package manager.

// Illustrative future schema
class Package {
    string name = "my_project"
    string version = "0.1.0"
    string description = "A sample firescript executable project"
    [string] authors = ["Jane Doe <jane@example.com>"]

    // The compiler this package is built for (defaults to "firescript")
    string compiler = "firescript"

    // Entry point of the package.
    // Defaults to "src/main.fire" for binaries or "src/lib.fire" for libraries.
    string entry = "src/main.fire"

    // Path to the license file
    string licenseFile = "LICENSE"
}

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
The dependencies section will specify packages and libraries your project requires to build and run. kiln will support semantic versioning (semver) for package resolution, plus local-path and Git dependencies:

  • A specific version from the default package registry (e.g., http = "1.2.0")
  • A local path relative to kiln.fcl
  • A Git repository URL with a branch or tag

docs.title: Development Dependencies
docs.text: Dev-dependencies list packages only required for testing or local development. These dependencies will not be bundled or forwarded when another project depends on your package.

docs.title: Build Profiles
docs.text: kiln will allow configuring default compiler arguments for different environments (dev and release profiles). If omitted, kiln falls back to sane defaults.

docs.title: Lockfile (kiln.lock)
docs.text:
When kiln resolves dependencies, it calculates hashes and exact semantic versions, writing them to an auto-generated kiln.lock file.

  • Reproducibility: This lockfile ensures that any user cloning your repository will build against the exact same dependency tree you did.
  • Do not edit manually: This file should only be managed by kiln.
  • Version control: It should typically be committed to version control for executable packages (bins).

docs.title: Expected Project Layout
docs.text:
kiln enforces standard conventions for directory layout to minimize the need for manual configuration.

my_project/
├── kiln.fcl           # The package manifest
├── kiln.lock          # Auto-generated lockfile (after running `kiln build`)
├── src/               # Main source code directory
│   ├── main.fire      # Default entry point for executables
│   └── lib.fire       # Default entry point for libraries
├── tests/             # Integration and unit tests
│   └── my_test.fire
└── build/             # Auto-generated build output directory (gitignored)

docs.title: kiln Package Manager
docs.text:
Status: [IN DEVELOPMENT]. kiln exists today as a bootstrap prototype written in firescript at firescript/tools/kiln/ (CLI skeleton with help text). The commands and behavior described on this page are the design target — most of it is [PLANNED], with the core commands (new/init, build, run, check, test, clean) scheduled for the 0.10.0 release and registry features (publish, add, sync, update) for 0.11.0. The manifest format is FCL (kiln.fcl), not TOML.

kiln is the official package manager, build system, and task runner for the firescript programming language. Its design is heavily inspired by tools like Cargo (Rust) and NPM (Node.js), providing a standardized, zero-configuration way to manage dependencies, compile your code, and run tests.

Instead of invoking the firescript compiler manually with dozens of complicated flags, kiln orchestrates the entire process.

docs.title: 1. Quick Start and CLI Commands [PLANNED]
docs.text: kiln provides a simple, verb-based Command Line Interface (CLI):

docs.title: Creating a New Project
docs.text:
# Create a new executable project
kiln new my_app

# Create a new library project
kiln new my_lib --lib

This generates the standard project layout and a base kiln.fcl manifest file.

docs.title: Building and Running
docs.text:
# Compile the project and output the binary to build/
kiln build

# Compile the project with optimizations (Release mode)
kiln build --release

# Compile and immediately run the executable
kiln run
# Pass arguments to the executable:
kiln run -- --my-arg value

# Type-check the project without emitting a binary (fast)
kiln check

docs.title: Testing and Maintenance
docs.text:
# Run all tests in the tests/ directory
kiln test

# Clean all generated artifacts from the build/ directory
kiln clean

docs.title: 2. Configuration (kiln.fcl) [PLANNED]
docs.text:
The kiln.fcl file defines your project's metadata, dependencies, and build profiles. It is written in FCL (firescript config language) and acts as the source of truth for the package manager, located at the root of your project.

See kiln Manifest for the manifest format details.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: The dependencies section specifies packages and libraries your project requires. kiln will support semantic versioning (semver) for package resolution as well as local/remote resolution — pulling a specific version from the default package registry, from a local path relative to kiln.fcl, or directly from a Git repository.

docs.title: Development Dependencies
docs.text: Dev-dependencies list packages only required for testing or local development. These dependencies will not be bundled or forwarded when another project depends on your package.

docs.title: Build Profiles
docs.text: kiln will allow configuring default compiler arguments for different environments (dev and release profiles). If omitted, kiln falls back to sane defaults.

docs.title: 3. The Lockfile (kiln.lock) [PLANNED]
docs.text:
When kiln resolves dependencies, it calculates hashes and exact semantic versions, writing them to an auto-generated kiln.lock file.

  • Reproducibility: This lockfile ensures that any user cloning your repository will build against the exact same dependency tree you did.
  • Do not edit manually: This file should only be managed by kiln.
  • Version control: It should typically be committed to version control for executable packages, but is often ignored for library packages (to test against the latest compatible upstream versions).

docs.title: 4. Expected Project Layout [PLANNED]
docs.text:
kiln enforces standard conventions for directory layout to minimize the need for manual configuration. As long as you follow this layout, kiln won't require overriding paths in kiln.fcl.

my_project/
├── kiln.fcl           # The package manifest
├── kiln.lock          # Auto-generated lockfile (after running `kiln build`)
├── src/               # Main source code directory
│   ├── main.fire      # Default entry point for executables
│   └── lib.fire       # Default entry point for libraries
├── tests/             # Integration and unit tests
│   └── my_test.fire
└── build/             # Auto-generated build output directory (gitignored)

docs.title: 5. Under the Hood: Compiler Discovery [PLANNED]
docs.text:
Because kiln orchestrates the firescript compiler to retrieve artifacts and dependencies, it needs to know where the compiler lives. kiln will resolve the compiler using a fallback chain:

  1. Environment Variable: FIRESCRIPT_COMPILER (e.g., FIRESCRIPT_COMPILER="python /path/to/main.py").
  2. Local Dev Fallback: If firescript/main.py or .venv exists adjacent to kiln, it hooks directly into the local repository (useful for bootstrapped language development!).
  3. PATH Fallback: Finally, it assumes the compiler was installed globally and invokes firescript from the PATH.

When kiln executes the compiler, it communicates via JSON diagnostics, making use of the --message-format=json, --emit, and --emit-deps CLI flags internally.

docs.title: Command-Line Arguments (std.cli.args)
docs.text: The std.cli.args module provides utilities for parsing and accessing command-line arguments in a robust, convention-friendly way.

docs.title: argc()
docs.text:
int32 argc()

Return the count of command-line arguments (including the program name).

docs.title: argv_at(int32 index)
docs.text:
string argv_at(int32 index)

Get the argument at the specified index. Index 0 is the program name.

docs.title: has_flag(string name)
docs.text:
bool has_flag(string name)

Check if a boolean flag was provided. Supports short form (-x) and long form (--flag), as well as grouped short flags (-abc).

Parameters: - name: Flag name (without the dash prefix)

Example:

import @firescript/std.cli.args.has_flag;

if (has_flag("verbose")) {
    println("Verbose mode enabled");
}

// Recognizes: -v, --verbose, or -xvyz (grouped flags)

docs.title: has_flag_alias(string long_name, string short_name)
docs.text:
bool has_flag_alias(string long_name, string short_name)

Check if a flag was provided using a long or short alias.

Example:

if (has_flag_alias("output", "o")) {
    println("Output flag recognized");
}

// Recognizes: -o, --output, or grouped forms

docs.title: option_value(string name, string fallback)
docs.text:
string option_value(string name, string fallback)

Get the value for an option. Supports multiple syntaxes: - -n value - --name value - -n=value - --name=value

Parameters: - name: Option name (without dashes) - fallback: Value to return if option not provided

Example:

import @firescript/std.cli.args.option_value;

string output = option_value("output", "stdout");

// Recognizes:
//   -o file.txt
//   --output file.txt
//   -o=file.txt
//   --output=file.txt

docs.title: option_value_alias(string long_name, string short_name, string fallback)
docs.text:
string option_value_alias(string long_name, string short_name, string fallback)

Get the value for an option providing long and short name aliases.

Example:

string port = option_value_alias("port", "p", "8080");

docs.title: positional(int32 index, string fallback)
docs.text:
string positional(int32 index, string fallback)

Get a positional argument by index (0-based, after the program name).

Example:

import @firescript/std.cli.args.positional;

string cmd = positional(0, "help");  // First positional arg

docs.title: positional_value(int32 index, string fallback)
docs.text:
string positional_value(int32 index, string fallback)

Get the nth positional value, skipping options and flags. Respects -- as the end of options.

Example:

// For: prog --verbose file1 file2
// positional_value(0, ...) returns "file1"
// positional_value(1, ...) returns "file2"

docs.title: Syntax Support
docs.text:
The module recognizes standard CLI conventions:

  • Long flags: --verbose, --flag
  • Short flags: -v, -f
  • Grouped short flags: -abc (equivalent to -a -b -c)
  • Options with values:
  • -n value
  • --name value
  • -n=value
  • --name=value
  • Grouped short with value: -abc (where b takes a value in certain contexts)
  • End of options: -- stops processing flags; all following arguments are positional

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import @firescript/std.cli.args.argc;
import @firescript/std.cli.args.argv_at;
import @firescript/std.cli.args.has_flag;
import @firescript/std.cli.args.option_value;
import @firescript/std.cli.args.positional_value;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

// Program: myapp -v --output result.txt file.txt

if (has_flag("verbose") || has_flag("v")) {
    println("Verbose mode");
}

string output = option_value("output", "a.out");
println("Output: " + output);

string input = positional_value(0, "stdin");
println("Input: " + input);

docs.title: File System (std.fs)
docs.text: The std.fs module provides file-system operations through an object-oriented File API centered on methods and result types.

docs.title: File
docs.text:
Represents a file path and provides methods for common file operations.

class File {
    string path;

    File(&mut this, string path);

    FileResult read(&this);
    FileResult readBytes(&this, int32 bytes);
    FileResult writeAll(&this, string data);
    FileResult appendAll(&this, string data);
    bool exists(&this);
    FileResult remove(&this);
    FileResult renameTo(&mut this, string new_path);
    FileResult moveTo(&mut this, string dst_path);
}

Constructor:

File(&mut this, string path)

Create a File instance for the given path. The path is stored but the file is not opened until an operation is called.

Parameters: - path: File path (relative or absolute)

Example:

import @firescript/std.fs.File;

File myFile = File("hello.txt");

docs.title: FileResult
docs.text:
Encapsulates the result of a file operation.

class FileResult {
    int32 status;
    string data;

    FileResult(int32 status, string data);

    bool ok(&this);
    int32 err_code(&this);
    int32 result_status(&this);
    string result_data(&this);
}

Fields: - status: Operation status (≥ 0 for success, negative errno for failure) - data: Operation output (e.g., file contents for reads), empty for most writes

Check the status: A negative status indicates an error. Use FileResult methods to inspect results.

docs.title: read()
docs.text:
FileResult read(&this)

Read the entire file contents into a string.

Returns: FileResult with status = bytes read (on success) or negative error code

Example:

import @firescript/std.fs.File;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

File f = File("data.txt");
FileResult r = f.read();
if (r.ok()) {
    println(r.result_data());
}

docs.title: readBytes(int32 bytes)
docs.text:
FileResult readBytes(&this, int32 bytes)

Read up to the specified number of bytes.

Parameters: - bytes: Maximum bytes to read

Returns: FileResult with status = bytes actually read

Example:

FileResult chunk = f.readBytes(1024);

docs.title: writeAll(string data)
docs.text:
FileResult writeAll(&this, string data)

Write data to the file, overwriting if it exists.

Parameters: - data: String data to write

Returns: FileResult with status = bytes written (on success) or error code

Example:

FileResult w = f.writeAll("Hello!");

docs.title: appendAll(string data)
docs.text:
FileResult appendAll(&this, string data)

Append data to the end of the file.

Parameters: - data: String data to append

Returns: FileResult with status = bytes written (on success) or error code

Example:

FileResult a = f.appendAll("\nMore text");

docs.title: exists()
docs.text:
bool exists(&this)

Check if the file exists and is readable.

Returns: true if file exists and can be opened for reading, false otherwise

Example:

if (f.exists()) {
    println("File found!");
}

docs.title: remove()
docs.text:
FileResult remove(&this)

Delete the file.

Returns: FileResult with status = 0 on success, negative error code on failure

Example:

f.remove();

docs.title: renameTo(string new_path)
docs.text:
FileResult renameTo(&mut this, string new_path)

Rename (or move within same filesystem) to a new path. Updates the File path on success.

Parameters: - new_path: New file path

Returns: FileResult with status = 0 on success

Example:

f.renameTo("newname.txt");

docs.title: moveTo(string dst_path)
docs.text:
FileResult moveTo(&mut this, string dst_path)

Move file to a new location with cross-filesystem fallback. On cross-device moves, automatically falls back to copy + delete. Updates the File path on success.

Parameters: - dst_path: Destination path

Returns: FileResult with status = 0 on success

Example:

f.moveTo("/other/location/file.txt");

docs.title: ok()
docs.text:
bool ok(&this)

Check if operation succeeded (status >= 0).

Example:

if (result.ok()) {
    println("Success!");
}

docs.title: err_code()
docs.text:
int32 err_code(&this)

Extract the error code (positive errno value). Returns 0 if operation succeeded.

Example:

int32 err = result.err_code();
if (err > 0) {
    println("Error: " + (err as string));
}

docs.title: result_status()
docs.text:
int32 result_status(&this)

Get the raw status field (positive on success, negative errno on failure).

docs.title: result_data()
docs.text:
string result_data(&this)

Get the data field (file contents for reads, empty for writes).

docs.title: Error Codes
docs.text:
File operations return negative errno values on error:

  • -2: ENOENT (file not found)
  • -13: EACCES (permission denied)
  • -28: ENOSPC (no space on device)
  • -30: EROFS (read-only filesystem)
  • etc.

Consult your system's errno.h for a complete list.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import @firescript/std.fs.File;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

File config = File("config.txt");

// Write
FileResult write_result = config.writeAll("setting=value");
if (write_result.ok()) {
    println("Configuration saved");
}

// Read
FileResult read_result = config.read();
if (read_result.ok()) {
    println("Configuration: " + read_result.result_data());
}

// Cleanup
config.remove();

docs.title: Standard I/O (std.io)
docs.text: The std.io module provides basic output utilities for printing values to standard output.

docs.title: print
docs.text:
void print<T>(&T value)

Print a value to standard output without a trailing newline. The value is implicitly converted to a string.

Parameters: - value: Any type (will be converted to string for output)

Example:

import @firescript/std.io.print;

print("Hello");
print(42);
print(3.14);

docs.title: println
docs.text:
void println<T>(&T value)

Print a value to standard output followed by a newline.

Parameters: - value: Any type (will be converted to string for output)

Example:

import @firescript/std.io.println;

println("Hello, World!");
println(100);

docs.title: Supported Types
docs.text:
Both print and println are generic and support all built-in types that can be converted to strings:

  • Numeric types (intN, uintN, floatN)
  • bool
  • char
  • string

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Both functions are generic and polymorphic; they infer the type from the argument.
  • Output goes directly to standard output (stdout).

docs.title: Math (std.math)
docs.text:
The std.math module provides basic mathematical utilities.

Status: abs, min, max, and clamp are exported and importable today. The module also contains trigonometry, sqrt, pow, angle-conversion helpers, and a large set of mathematical constants, but those are not yet exported and cannot be imported from user code — they are listed at the bottom of this page as [IN DEVELOPMENT].

docs.title: Numeric Constraint
docs.text:
The exported functions are generic over the Numeric constraint, a type union covering all integer and floating-point types:

Numeric = int8 | int16 | int32 | int64 | uint8 | uint16 | uint32 | uint64 | float32 | float64 | float128

Because the functions are generic, all arguments in a call must share the same type.

docs.title: abs(T x, T zero)
docs.text:
Return the absolute value of x. The second argument supplies the zero value for the type (e.g., 0 for int32, 0.0 for float64).

import @firescript/std.math.abs;

int32 v = abs(-42, 0);           // 42
float64 f = abs(-3.14, 0.0);     // 3.14

docs.title: min(T a, T b)
docs.text:
Return the smaller of two values.

import @firescript/std.math.min;

int32 result = min(5, 3);        // 3

docs.title: max(T a, T b)
docs.text:
Return the larger of two values.

import @firescript/std.math.max;

int32 result = max(5, 3);        // 5

docs.title: clamp(T x, T lo, T hi)
docs.text:
Constrain x to the range [lo, hi].

import @firescript/std.math.clamp;

int32 result = clamp(10, 0, 5);  // 5

You can also import several symbols at once:

import @firescript/std.math.{max, min, abs, clamp}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import @firescript/std.math.{max, min, clamp};
import @firescript/std.io.println;

int32 biggest = max(3, 7);
float64 smallest = min(2.5, 1.5);
int32 bounded = clamp(15, 0, 10);

println(biggest);   // 7
println(smallest);  // 1.5
println(bounded);   // 10

Note: assign the result of a generic call to a variable before passing it to another generic function (like println). Nesting generic calls directly (e.g., println(max(3, 7))) currently fails to compile — a known limitation.

docs.title: Not Yet Exported [IN DEVELOPMENT]
docs.text:
The following exist in the module sources but are private (not exported), so they cannot be imported yet:

  • Functions (firescript/std/math/init.fire): sqrt, pow, pow_int, sin, cos, tan, lerp, deg_to_rad, rad_to_deg
  • Constants (firescript/std/math/constants.fire): precision-specific constants for pi (PI32/PI64/PI128, plus PI_2_*, PI_4_*, INV_PI*, SQRT_PI*, LN_PI*), Euler's number (E*, LOG2_E*, LOG10_E*), natural logs (LN_2_*, LN_10_*), square roots (SQRT_2_*, SQRT_3_*, SQRT_1_2_*), special constants (EULER_GAMMA*, PHI*, CATALAN*), and degree/radian conversion multipliers (DEG_TO_RAD*, RAD_TO_DEG*)

These will be documented here once they are exported and tested.

docs.title: Standard Library Overview
docs.text: firescript includes a standard library under the @firescript/std namespace, providing utilities for common programming tasks.

docs.title: Guide to Modules
docs.text:
• I/O: Formatted output with print and println
  • File System: File operations via the File class and FileResult helpers
  • Math: Mathematical functions
  • Regex: Regular-expression matching, anchors, and pattern validation
  • Command-Line Arguments: Argument parsing for CLI programs
  • Types: Generic container types (Tuple, Option)
  • Ranges: range, rangeFrom, and rangeStep generators for counting loops (@firescript/std.ranges)
  • FCL Lexer: Lexer for parsing firescript config language (FCL) data (@firescript/std.fcl)

docs.title: Importing
docs.text:
Standard library modules are imported using @firescript/std namespace syntax:

import @firescript/std.io.println;
import @firescript/std.fs.File;
import @firescript/std.math.max;
import @firescript/std.fcl.next_token;  // FCL lexer

You can also import at the module level:

import @firescript/std.io;  // imports all I/O exports
import @firescript/std.fcl;  // imports all FCL lexer functions

Or use wildcard imports:

import @firescript/std.types.*;  // imports Tuple, Option, etc.

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text: Most file-system operations return result types (e.g., FileResult) that encode success/failure in a status field. Check the File System module for details on error semantics and FileResult methods like ok().

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Prefer stdlib over syscalls: User code should use standard library modules, not low-level syscalls (which require directive enable_syscalls).
  • Explicit imports: Import specific symbols to avoid namespace pollution and make dependencies clear.
  • Check result status: Always inspect result types after file operations to handle errors robustly.

docs.title: std.regex
docs.text:
@firescript/std.regex provides a small, pure firescript regular-expression helper module.

This page covers the current API and syntax that the module supports today, and leaves room for future sections on captures, search helpers, replacements, and richer pattern syntax.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
import @firescript/std.io.println;
import @firescript/std.regex.is_match;
import @firescript/std.regex.find_at;
import @firescript/std.regex.last_error;

println(is_match("(ab)+c", "ababc"));
println(is_match("[a-z]+", "firescript"));
println(is_match("^fire", "firescript"));
println(find_at("script", "firescript", 4));
println(last_error("[ab"));

docs.title: bool is_match(string pattern, string text)
docs.text: Returns true when the pattern matches the input text. Without anchors, the pattern must match the entire input; ^ and $ control anchoring explicitly.

docs.title: bool find_match(string pattern, string text)
docs.text: Alias of is_match. Named match prior to 0.6.0; renamed because match is now a reserved keyword.

docs.title: int32 find_at(string pattern, string text, int32 start_pos)
docs.text: Position-aware matching: returns the length of the match starting at start_pos, or -1 if there is no match at that position.

docs.title: string last_error(string pattern)
docs.text: Validates a pattern and returns an empty string when the pattern is usable. If validation fails, it returns a short error message.

docs.title: RegexPattern
docs.text:
The RegexPattern class (@firescript/std.regex.pattern.RegexPattern) lets you construct a pattern once and match it repeatedly:

import @firescript/std.regex.pattern.RegexPattern;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

RegexPattern p = RegexPattern("[a-z]+");
println(p.is_match("firescript"));
println(p.find_at("firescript", 4));

Methods:

  • bool is_match(&this, string &text) — match against the text
  • bool matches(&this, string &text) — alias of is_match
  • int32 find_at(&this, string &text, int32 start_pos) — position-aware matching
  • string last_error(&this) — pattern validation error, or empty string

docs.title: Supported Syntax
docs.text:
The current matcher supports:

  • Literals like abc
  • Escapes like \n, \t, \r, and escaped metacharacters
  • Wildcard .
  • Anchors ^ (start) and $ (end)
  • Grouping with ( ... )
  • Alternation with |
  • Quantifiers *, +, and ?
  • Character classes like [abc], [^abc], and simple ranges like [a-z]

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Pattern errors are reported at runtime via last_error; there is no compile-time pattern validation yet.
  • Counted quantifiers ({n,m}), lookarounds, non-greedy quantifiers, captures, and backreferences are not supported yet.
  • Character-class range ordering is intentionally simple and currently best suited to ASCII alphanumeric ranges.

docs.title: Future Sections [PLANNED]
docs.text:
These are good follow-up topics for later expansion:

  • Capturing groups and submatch extraction
  • Search helpers like find_first and find_all
  • Replacement helpers
  • More complete character classes and escaping rules

docs.title: Generic Types (std.types)
docs.text:
The std.types module provides generic container and utility types.

Status: Tuple, CopyableTuple, Option, and CopyableOption are all [IMPLEMENTED] and tested.

docs.title: Tuple<T, U>
docs.text:
A pair of values of potentially different types.

class Tuple<T, U> {
    T first;
    U second;

    Tuple(T first, U second);
}

Example:

import @firescript/std.types.Tuple;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

Tuple<int32, string> pair = Tuple<int32, string>(42, "answer");
println(pair.first);   // 42
println(pair.second);  // answer

Note: Tuple is an Owned type; values are moved, not copied.

docs.title: CopyableTuple<T, U>
docs.text:
A copyable variant of Tuple for use with copyable types only.

copyable class CopyableTuple<T, U> {
    T first;
    U second;

    CopyableTuple(T first, U second);
}

Example:

import @firescript/std.types.CopyableTuple;

CopyableTuple<float64, float64> coord = CopyableTuple<float64, float64>(3.14, 2.71);
CopyableTuple<float64, float64> copy = coord;  // copies

docs.title: Option<T> [IMPLEMENTED]
docs.text:
An optional value of type T. Use this to represent potentially missing values without explicit nullability.

class Option<T?> {
    T value?;

    Option(T value?);

    bool isSome();
    bool isNone();
}

Methods:

  • isSome(): Returns true if a value is present
  • isNone(): Returns true if no value is present

Example:

Intended usage (see the known issue above):

import @firescript/std.types.Option;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

Option<string> maybe_name = Option<string>("Alice");
bool present = maybe_name.isSome();
if (present) {
    println(maybe_name.value);
}

Note: Option is an Owned type; construct carefully with move semantics in mind.

docs.title: CopyableOption<T> [IMPLEMENTED]
docs.text:
A copyable variant of Option for copyable types.

copyable class CopyableOption<T?> {
    T value?;

    CopyableOption(T value?);

    bool isSome();
    bool isNone();
}

Example:

Intended usage (see the known issue above):

import @firescript/std.types.CopyableOption;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

CopyableOption<int32> maybe_count = CopyableOption<int32>(5);
bool present = maybe_count.isSome();
if (present) {
    println(maybe_count.value);
}

docs.title: Usage Notes
docs.text:
• Prefer CopyableTuple and CopyableOption for numeric/scalar types to avoid unnecessary moves.
  • Use owned variants for containers or strings that you want to pass around differently.
  • Both variants support generic type parameters; compile-time monomorphization produces efficient code.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import @firescript/std.types.Tuple;
import @firescript/std.io.println;

// Tuple usage
Tuple<int32, string> user = Tuple<int32, string>(1, "alice");
println("User ID: " + (user.first as string));
println(user.second);
